region_of_origin: South India; widely consumed in Singapore and Malaysia

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for muruku exists; mapped to broader snack food product class. Muruku is a deep-fried extruded snack made from lentil and tapioca flour.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (extruded/expanded snacks) is the closest defensible parent match for muruku, a deep-fried extruded lentil-flour snack. No exact muruku-specific FoodEx2 code exists; this is an analogous closest-parent assignment.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for muruku or equivalent fried lentil snack product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of muruku is approximately 30 g (a small handful), providing around 150 kcal, 9 g fat, and 14 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Muruku — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Muruku (F11090039). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for muruku exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 72 (high) based on the closest analogous food: deep-fried rice crackers / extruded lentil-flour snacks with similar carbohydrate profile (GI ~70–75 per Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Glycaemic load per 30 g serving = 72 × (46.2 × 0.30) / 100 = 10.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 811 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit muruku; a single 30 g serving delivers approximately 243 mg sodium, representing over 10% of the 2000 mg daily limit, and regular consumption will significantly elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (~72), 46.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, and 30.1 g fat from deep-frying impair postprandial glucose control and insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid muruku as a regular snack; the high GI, dense carbohydrate load, and fat from deep-frying combine to cause rapid blood glucose spikes and worsen insulin resistance, and only very small occasional portions should be considered.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~8.5 g/100 g, trans fat ~0.2 g/100 g, sodium 811 mg/100 g, and high caloric density 500 kcal/100 g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid muruku; the combination of saturated fat, trans fat from deep-frying, and very high sodium contributes to dyslipidaemia, arterial stiffness, and elevated blood pressure, all of which are primary cardiovascular risk factors.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); trans fat ~0.2 g/100 g and saturated fat ~8.5 g/100 g exceed RA thresholds
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid muruku; deep-frying produces AGEs and trans fats that are strongly pro-inflammatory, and the high saturated fat and sodium content further exacerbate joint inflammation and tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Muruku (also spelled Murukku) is a traditional South Indian deep-fried savoury snack widely consumed in Singapore, Malaysia, and the Indian subcontinent, particularly within Tamil and Mamak culinary traditions. It is made by combining dhal flour (lentil flour) and tapioca flour with coconut milk, salt, and spices such as cumin and sesame seeds to form a stiff dough, which is then extruded through a star-shaped or spiral die and deep-fried in hot oil until golden and crisp. The result is a crunchy, spiral-shaped snack with a savoury, mildly spiced flavour and a light, airy yet firm texture. Muruku is a calorie-dense food at 500 kcal per 100 g, with 30.1 g fat (including approximately 8.5 g saturated fat and an estimated 0.2 g trans fat from frying), 46.2 g carbohydrate, 10.9 g protein, 4.7 g dietary fibre, and a very high sodium content of 811 mg per 100 g. Its glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 72 (high), making it unsuitable for blood glucose management. Clinically, muruku is a high-concern food for hypertension (sodium 811 mg/100 g), type 2 diabetes (high GI, high carbohydrate, high fat), cardiovascular disease (saturated fat, trans fat, sodium), and rheumatoid arthritis (pro-inflammatory AGEs from deep-frying). It is vegetarian and typically halal, and is gluten-free by ingredient composition. In Singapore, muruku is a popular festive snack during Deepavali and is commonly sold at mamak stalls and Indian provision shops year-round.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of muruku?
Can I eat muruku if I have high blood pressure?
What is the Tamil name for muruku?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F11090039) with confidence 0.95. This is the canonical primary source for Singapore food records. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed reference, clearly documented. Saturated fat and trans fat are estimated from the deep-frying profile and ingredient composition, documented in notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (8.5 g/100 g) is estimated from the ingredient and frying profile of muruku (coconut milk base, deep-fried in vegetable oil); the HPB Foodo entry does not separately report saturated fat. Trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) is estimated from deep-frying in partially hydrogenated or repeatedly heated oil; this is consistent with the input record's health suitability notes. Both values are flagged as estimates and should be replaced with laboratory-confirmed values when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this traditional deep-fried preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry F11090039 as of April 2026. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on deep-fried extruded lentil/rice flour snacks per Atkinson FS et al. (2008), Diabetes Care; no direct muruku GI measurement was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycaemic load per 30 g serving = 72 × (46.2 × 0.30) / 100 = 10.0. 4. The food is classified as vegetarian (contains no meat or fish) but not vegan due to coconut milk. Halal status is assumed based on ingredient composition (no pork, no alcohol); formal halal certification should be verified for commercial products. 5. Chinese names (米饼干 / 米餅乾) are used as descriptive approximations meaning 'rice cracker/biscuit'; muruku does not have a single universally standardised Chinese name in Singapore usage. The Tamil name முறுக்கு is the canonical origin-language name. 6. FoodEx2 match type set to 'closest_parent' as A0FJP (extruded/expanded snacks) is the nearest parent category; no exact FoodEx2 code for muruku exists.
